prs.title: webhooks: acknowledge provider callbacks instantly, process via queue
prs.body:
## Summary

The provider callback endpoint now only validates the signature, persists the raw event, and returns 204 in <50ms. A queue worker performs the state transition and ledger write with an at-most-once guard keyed on the provider event id.

Motivated by the incident in issue #13 — the "why" (the delivery-deadline contract) lives there, not in this diff.

## Changes

- `webhooks/views.py`: verify → persist → 204; no processing in the handler
- `webhooks/tasks.py`: worker with `event_id` dedupe guard (unique constraint)
- `webhooks/models.py`: `RawEvent` table (payload, signature, processed flag)

## Verification

| Scenario | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Ack time under load | 10–24s | 31ms p99 |
| Same event delivered twice | processed twice | second is a no-op |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-74
- component: webhooks_service
prs.author: Notchayan

prs.title: search: serve catalog queries from the denormalized read table
prs.body:
## Summary

Catalog search now reads exclusively from `search_read_model`, a precomputed denormalized table refreshed by a sync job within 60s of catalog changes. Search no longer joins normalized tables at request time.

Motivated by the incident in issue #12 — the "why" (what live fan-out reads did to the primary) lives there, not in this diff.

## Changes

- `search/read_model.py`: flat read table (product, price, stock, category path)
- `search/views.py`: query the read table only; drop the ORM join chain
- `jobs/sync_read_model.py`: incremental sync on catalog change events

## Benchmarks

| Metric | Joins (live) | Read table |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| Search p95 | 6,100ms | 38ms |
| Primary CPU at search peak | 95% | 12% |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-72
- component: search_service
prs.author: Notchayan

prs.title: infra: route all Postgres access through PgBouncer (transaction pooling)
prs.body:
## Summary

Application workers no longer open direct database connections. All access flows through PgBouncer in transaction-pooling mode with server connections capped at 40, decoupling app-tier scale from database session capacity.

Motivated by the incident in issue #11 — the "why" (the autoscale blast radius) lives there, not in this diff.

## Changes

- `infra/pgbouncer.ini`: transaction mode, `default_pool_size=40`, TLS passthrough
- `config/settings.py`: `DATABASES` host → pooler endpoint; disable server-side cursors
- `docker-compose.yml`: `pgbouncer` service in front of `postgres:16`
- `docs/runbooks/db-pooling.md`: sizing + failure modes

## Verification (staging, 96 workers)

| Metric | Direct | Pooled |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Server sessions at peak | 384 (refused) | 40 (stable) |
| Checkout availability during autoscale | 503s | 100% |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-70
- component: infra, orders_service
prs.author: Notchayan

prs.title: payments: require idempotency keys on the capture API
prs.body:
## Summary

Every capture request now carries a client-generated idempotency key. Keys persist in `payments_idempotency` with a unique constraint; a replayed key returns the stored original result without touching the processor.

Motivated by the incident in issue #10 — the "why" (what a retry burst did to real customers) lives there, not in this diff.

## Changes

- `payments/capture.py`: reject captures without a key; replay-safe result return
- `payments/models.py`: `IdempotencyRecord` (key unique, response snapshot, TTL 48h)
- `sdk/client.py`: SDK generates a UUIDv4 key per logical charge

## Verification

| Scenario | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Same capture sent 3x concurrently | 3 charges | 1 charge, 2 replays |
| Processor timeout + client retry | double charge | single charge |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-69
- component: payments_service
prs.author: Notchayan

prs.title: auth: validate requests with stateless signed tokens
prs.body:
## Summary

Replaces database-backed session validation with self-contained signed tokens (HMAC, 15-minute expiry, silent refresh). The auth middleware now verifies the signature in-process; no storage round-trip on any request.

Motivated by the incident in issue #9 — the "why" (the load numbers, the revocation tradeoff) lives there, not in this diff.

## Changes

- `auth/tokens.py`: token mint/verify (HMAC-SHA256, `exp`, `iat`, key id)
- `auth/middleware.py`: validate from signature; drop the session-store lookup
- `config/settings.py`: remove `django.contrib.sessions` from the request path
- `auth/refresh.py`: sliding refresh endpoint (rotates before expiry)

## Benchmarks

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Auth check | 9.8ms (DB read) | 40µs (in-process) |
| Primary DB load share from auth | ~70% (burst) | 0% |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-64
- component: auth_service
prs.author: Notchayan

prs.title: feat: async email dispatch via Redis/Celery queue
prs.body:
## Summary

Moves order-confirmation email off the checkout request path onto a Redis/Celery queue.
The checkout endpoint now returns 200 immediately after the order is written; a Celery
worker handles SMTP dispatch with exponential backoff (3 retries, max 60s delay).

Motivated by the incident in issue #8 — the "why" (latency math, conversion cost,
the tradeoff we accepted) lives there, not in this diff.

## Changes

- `email_service/tasks.py`: new Celery task `send_order_confirmation(order_id)`
- `checkout/views.py`: remove direct `send_email()` call; replace with `send_order_confirmation.delay(order.id)`
- `email_service/worker.py`: Celery app config with Redis broker
- `reconciliation/jobs.py`: daily job to resend missing confirmation emails (the safety net)
- `docker-compose.yml`: add `celery-worker` and `celery-beat` services

## Benchmarks

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Checkout p95 latency | 1,340ms | 541ms |
| Email delivery rate | 99.1% (sync) | 99.7% (async + retry) |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-91-blackfriday-checkout-latency.md
- component: email_service
prs.author: Notchayan

prs.title: chore: migrate orders + payments to PostgreSQL
prs.body:
## Summary

Replaces the MongoDB collections (`orders_v1`, `payments_v1`) with PostgreSQL tables.
All financial and order-state data now lives in Postgres with full ACID semantics and
DB-level foreign-key constraints.

Motivated by the data-integrity incident in issue #7 — the "why" (the chaos-test
corruption numbers, the compliance requirement, the tradeoff) lives there, not in this diff.
Mechanically: a maintenance-window migration (export Mongo → transform → bulk-insert
Postgres), not a dual-write — dual-write doubles the failure surface and leaves two sources
of truth.

## Changes

- `orders/models.py`: Django ORM models for `Order`, `OrderLine`, `Refund`
- `payments/models.py`: `Payment`, `PaymentEvent` tables with FK constraints to `Order`
- `migrations/0031_orders_to_pg.py`: Alembic migration scripts
- `orders/repositories.py`, `payments/repositories.py`: replace PyMongo queries with SQLAlchemy
- `docker-compose.yml`: swap `mongo` service for `postgres:16`
- `scripts/migrate_mongo_to_pg.py`: one-time data migration (run once, delete after)
- `tests/`: updated fixtures; added transaction-rollback tests for payment failure paths

## Benchmark (staging, 500k order records)

| Metric | MongoDB | PostgreSQL |
|--------|---------|------------|
| Order + Payment write (2-table atomic) | ~3.1ms (app-level "transaction") | 2.4ms (real transaction) |
| Refund with FK integrity check | N/A (app-enforced) | 2.8ms (DB-enforced) |
| Corrupt write rate (chaos test) | 0.4% silent bad state | 0% (constraint rejected) |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-58-mongo-transaction-integrity.md
- component: orders_service, payments_service
prs.author: Notchayan

prs.title: ops: add gateway-level rate limiting (Nginx limit_req)
prs.body:
## Summary

Adds `limit_req_zone` rules to the Nginx gateway and **removes** the emergency
`django-ratelimit` decorators from `/search` and `/products`. Rate limiting is now
enforced in exactly one place: the gateway.

Motivated by the duplicated-limits incident in issue #6 — the "why" (the scraping
post-mortem, the two-layers-double-counting failure, the edge-vs-app reasoning) lives
there, not in this diff.

## Changes

- `infra/nginx/api.conf`: `limit_req_zone` on `$binary_remote_addr`; `/search` capped at
  30 req/s per IP (burst=10); `/api/` global at 100 req/s per IP (burst=50)
- `infra/nginx/errors.conf`: custom 429 JSON body with `Retry-After` header
- `docker-compose.yml`: dev gateway service (`nginx:alpine`) with config hot-reload
- `requirements.txt`: remove `django-ratelimit==4.1.0`
- `search/views.py`, `products/views.py`: **remove** the emergency `@ratelimit` decorators
  (the gateway owns this now — do not re-add them)
- `docs/runbooks/rate-limit-tuning.md`: adjusting limits without a code deploy

## Load test (locust, 500 virtual users, 10s ramp)

| Scenario | Without gateway limit | With gateway limit |
|----------|-----------------------|--------------------|
| Peak req/s reaching Django workers | 11,800 | 312 |
| Worker pool saturation | Yes (100%) | No (28%) |
| p99 latency for legit users | 8,100ms | 420ms |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-27-duplicated-ratelimit-incident.md
- component: api_gateway
prs.author: Notchayan

issues.title: Provider callback timeouts caused duplicate order-state transitions
issues.body:
## Summary

Our payment provider delivers events by HTTP callback and expects a response within 10 seconds. We were doing the full event processing — state transition, ledger write, notification fan-out — inline in the request handler. Under load, handling crossed the deadline, the provider marked delivery failed and **re-delivered the same events**, which we then processed again: orders jumped states twice, and the ledger recorded double entries.

## What we measured

| Signal | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Inline handling time under load | 10–24s |
| Provider re-deliveries in the window | 312 |
| Orders with duplicated state transitions | 58 |
| Ledger corrections required | 58 manual entries |

## Root cause

The callback endpoint conflated *acknowledging receipt* with *doing the work*. The provider only needs the first; we made it wait on the second, and its timeout+retry contract turned our slowness into duplicate deliveries.

## The lesson / what we decided

Callback endpoints **acknowledge instantly and defer the work**: validate the signature, persist the raw event, return 204 in under 50ms; a queue worker processes events with an at-most-once guard keyed on the provider's event id. Receipt and processing are separate steps with separate clocks.

**Do not move event processing back into the callback request handler.** The provider's deadline is a contract; inline work will breach it under load every time.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-75-webhook-queue-ack.md
- assumption: inline processing time under load exceeds the provider's delivery deadline; holds while processing does synchronous writes/fan-out
- component: webhooks_service
issues.author: Notchayan

issues.title: Catalog search drove the primary to 95% CPU with multi-way join storms
issues.body:
## Summary

Catalog search was executing an 11-way join across normalized product, pricing, inventory and category tables — per keystroke, per shopper. During the holiday preview event the primary hit 95% CPU, search p95 reached 6.1s, and unrelated order writes slowed with it.

## What we measured

| Signal | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Joins per search query | 11 tables |
| Search p95 during the event | 6,100ms |
| Primary CPU | 95% sustained |
| Order-write latency degradation | 3.4x |

## Root cause

Read traffic with fan-out characteristics (search) was served from the same normalized schema that exists to keep writes correct. The normalized shape is right for writes and wrong for search — every search paid the full join cost live.

## The lesson / what we decided

Search reads come from a **precomputed denormalized read table**, refreshed by a sync job within 60 seconds of catalog changes. Search never joins the normalized tables at query time. Tradeoff accepted: up to a minute of staleness in search results — measured as invisible to shoppers, unlike 6-second queries.

**Search queries must not join the normalized catalog tables at request time.** "Fresher results" via live joins is how the primary melted.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-73-search-read-model.md
- assumption: live multi-table joins at search volume exceed primary capacity; holds while search QPS is within an order of magnitude of catalog write QPS
- component: search_service
issues.author: Notchayan

issues.title: Postgres refused new clients when autoscaling burst past the connection ceiling
issues.body:
## Summary

An autoscaling event during a marketing push doubled our web workers. Each worker opened its own database connections; the total blew past the server's `max_connections=100` ceiling and Postgres began refusing new clients (`FATAL: sorry, too many clients already`). Checkout returned 503s for 11 minutes while we manually scaled workers back down.

## What we measured

| Signal | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Worker count after autoscale | 96 (from 48) |
| Connections attempted | 384 (4 per worker) |
| Server ceiling | 100 |
| Checkout 503 window | 11 minutes |

## Root cause

Connection count scaled with worker count, but the database's capacity does not. Direct per-worker connections tie two numbers together that must be independent: how many app processes we run, and how many sessions the database can hold.

## The lesson / what we decided

All application database access goes through **PgBouncer in transaction-pooling mode**: workers share a small, fixed set of server connections (capped at 40) no matter how far the app tier scales. The app can autoscale freely; the database sees a constant, safe session count.

**Do not point application workers directly at Postgres.** Any config change that bypasses the pooler re-couples app scale to database capacity — the exact coupling that took checkout down.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-71-pgbouncer-transaction-pooling.md
- assumption: app-tier instance count is unbounded relative to database session capacity; holds while autoscaling exists
- component: orders_service, infra
issues.author: Notchayan

issues.title: Customers double-charged during the payment-gateway retry storm
issues.body:
## Summary

A 90-second latency spike at our card processor caused client SDKs and our own retry logic to re-send capture requests. **137 customers were charged twice** for the same order before we halted captures. Refunding took three days and produced a measurable spike in chargebacks and support contacts.

## What we measured

| Signal | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Double-captures during the window | 137 |
| Chargebacks traced to the event | 22 |
| Support tickets in 48h | 400+ |
| Refund + penalty cost | low five figures |

## Root cause

The capture endpoint treated every request as new work. Two identical capture requests — one original, one retry racing its timeout — both reached the processor. Nothing in our layer could recognize the second request as a replay of the first.

## The lesson / what we decided

Every money-moving request must carry a **client-generated idempotency key**, persisted with a unique constraint; a replayed key returns the original result instead of re-executing the charge. The store enforces at-most-once capture — not hopeful client code, not timing luck.

**Removing the idempotency-key requirement from any money-moving endpoint is banned.** A retry storm is not a hypothetical: we have the invoice.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-68-payment-idempotency-keys.md
- assumption: upstream timeouts make duplicate capture requests inevitable; holds while any client or gateway retries on timeout
- component: payments_service
issues.author: Notchayan

issues.title: Login path saturated the primary database during the flash-sale event
issues.body:
## Summary

During the spring flash-sale, the login and every authenticated request performed a row lookup against the `django_session` table. Session reads became **70% of all primary-database load**; p99 on sign-in climbed to 4.2s and several checkout workers stalled waiting for connections that session lookups were hogging.

## What we measured

| Signal | Value during the window |
|--------|------------------------|
| Share of primary DB load from session reads | ~70% |
| p99 sign-in latency | 4,200ms |
| Authenticated requests doing a session-table read | 100% (every request) |
| Checkout worker stalls from starved connections | 14 incidents in 40 min |

## Root cause

Server-side session state means every authenticated request pays a database round-trip before any business logic runs. Under burst traffic the hottest table in the system was pure bookkeeping, competing with revenue-bearing queries.

## The lesson / what we decided

Request authentication must be verifiable **without touching the database**: a signed, self-contained credential (HMAC token, 15-minute expiry, silent refresh) that the middleware validates in microseconds from the signature alone. Tradeoff accepted eyes-open: instant server-side revocation is gone — the short expiry bounds the exposure window, and a compromised account is locked by rotating the signing key.

**Do not reintroduce a per-request server-side state lookup into the auth path.** If someone wants instant revocation back, they must first show the primary database surviving flash-sale load with the bookkeeping reads restored.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-66-stateless-auth-tokens.md
- assumption: per-request session-state reads compete with revenue queries for the primary; holds while auth state lives in the request-serving database
- component: auth_service
issues.author: Notchayan

issues.title: Black Friday peak-traffic latency regression on the purchase path
issues.body:
## Summary

During Black Friday peak-traffic, the storefront purchase path degraded badly. p95
on the buy flow climbed to 1,340ms and held there for most of the high-traffic
window. This was our single worst conversion event of the year, and it traced back to
work we were doing *inside* the buy flow that did not belong there.

## What we measured

Profiling the slow traffic showed that ~800ms of the 1,340ms p95 was a single blocking
call: the buy flow was waiting on a third-party mail provider API before it could reply
to the shopper. The shopper sat staring at a spinner while we talked to an external
provider that had nothing to do with completing the purchase.

The business cost was real, not theoretical:

| Signal | Value during the window |
|--------|-------------------------|
| p95 on the buy flow | 1,340ms |
| Portion attributable to the blocking provider call | ~800ms |
| Drop in purchase completions past the 1.2s mark (from last quarter's A/B) | ~6% |
| Estimated lost conversion revenue over the window | mid five figures |

The A/B data from the previous quarter had already told us that completions fall sharply
once the buy flow crosses ~1.2s. Black Friday simply pushed us far past that cliff at
the exact moment traffic — and revenue at stake — was highest.

## Root cause

The mail provider call lived on the critical path of the buy flow. Any slowness on the
provider's side, or any traffic spike that lengthened our own processing, was paid for
straight away in shopper-perceived latency and lost conversion. The provider's
responsiveness is outside our control, yet it was sitting on the most revenue-sensitive
flow we serve.

## The lesson / what we decided

The mail provider call must come off the critical path. The shopper should get an instant
reply the moment the purchase is durably recorded; everything the shopper does not need
to wait for (notifying them by mail) happens afterward, out of band, with retry and
observability so a transient provider failure never silently loses a notification.

Tradeoff we accepted, eyes open: we give up the same-transaction guarantee that the mail
went out at the instant of purchase. A daily reconciliation sweep resends anything missing
older than a few minutes. We judged that loss of an in-band guarantee a fair price for
removing ~800ms from the most conversion-sensitive flow we own — and for no longer
being hostage to a third party's reply time during our highest-revenue traffic peaks.

**Do not move this work back onto the buy flow's critical path without re-reading this
incident.** The latency math and the Black Friday conversion loss are the whole point of
keeping it off the critical path. If someone argues the provider is "fast enough now," that
is an assumption to measure and ratify, not assume — the provider's latency is not ours to
promise.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-42-implement-async-email.md
- assumption: provider-on-critical-path costs ~800ms; holds until provider call latency drops materially or leaves the revenue-sensitive flow
- component: email_service
issues.author: Notchayan

issues.title: Silent data corruption in orders/payments under network partition
issues.body:
## Summary

Our chaos-test sprint surfaced a class of failure we cannot tolerate for money data:
under simulated network partition, the MongoDB write path for `orders` and `payments`
left records in an inconsistent state — an order recorded with no matching payment row,
and refunds that applied partially — with **nothing in the system complaining.** No error,
no alert. Just quietly wrong financial state that would have required manual reconciliation
to even find.

## What we measured

We ran the v2 data-layer redesign through a fault-injection harness (partition the app
from the store mid-write, repeat across the order + payment two-write sequence):

| Signal | MongoDB write path |
|--------|--------------------|
| Writes ending in inconsistent state (chaos run) | 0.4% |
| Orphaned records (order with no payment, or vice versa) | present |
| Partially-applied refunds | present |
| Surfaced as an error to the application | No — silent |
| Recovery | manual reconciliation only |

0.4% sounds small until you multiply it by payment volume. Every one of those is a
customer-trust event and a compliance problem, not an eventual-consistency footnote you
can shrug off.

## Root cause

The store gave us no way to make the two writes (order, then payment) atomic, and enforced
no integrity between them. Multi-document atomicity would have required the 4.0+
multi-document transaction API — extra overhead and *still* no foreign-key integrity — or
an application-level saga, which is far more machinery than the alternative. The "flexible
schema" we liked early on turned out to mean "the store will happily persist malformed,
half-finished financial state and tell us nothing."

## The lesson / what we decided

For transactional money data — orders, payments, refunds — correctness is not negotiable
and must be enforced **by the store, not by hopeful application code.** We want the store
to *reject* a half-written transaction, to refuse an orphaned record, to make the two-write
sequence all-or-nothing. The schema being strict is the feature: it is a contract the data
layer enforces on our behalf.

A standards-based audit trail (WAL-based change-data-capture) is also a hard requirement
for the upcoming PCI-DSS audit in Q3; the auditors expect a conventional setup, not a
bespoke change-stream explanation.

Tradeoff we accepted: migration scripts on every model change (auto-generated, ~30s of
review) and harder horizontal write-scaling. At ~400 writes/minute peak with comfortable
headroom, that scaling ceiling is years away.

**This decision is specifically for transactional financial data.** Product catalog,
search, and analytics event stores can absolutely use a document store. But if anyone
proposes moving orders or payments back onto a schemaless document store, they have to
disprove the 0.4% chaos-test corruption number first.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-31-migrate-to-postgres.md
- assumption: a schemaless store silently persists half-finished money state; holds until store-enforced multi-entity atomicity + integrity exists
- component: orders_service, payments_service
issues.author: Notchayan

issues.title: Legitimate users throttled by two rate-limit layers double-counting
issues.body:
## Summary

A scraping burst (~12k req/s from 3 IPs against `/search`) saturated our workers for 22
minutes; p99 hit 8.1s and conversion dropped ~31% during the window. As an emergency patch
we added `@ratelimit` decorators to the search and products views. They stopped the bleeding
— but within a day they created a second, self-inflicted incident: as we started rolling out
edge limits at the gateway, **two enforcement layers were counting the same requests
independently**, and legitimate users started getting rejected at roughly half the intended
allowance, inconsistently and unpredictably.

## What we measured

| Signal | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Scraping burst that triggered the patch | ~12k req/s from 3 IPs |
| Worker saturation during the burst | 22 minutes, p99 8.1s |
| Conversion drop during the burst window | ~31% |
| Effective allowance once both layers were live | ~50% of the intended limit |
| User-visible symptom | inconsistent rejections; a request "allowed" at one layer, rejected at the other |
| Reproducibility of the rejections | low — depended on which layer counted first |

The two layers had no shared notion of a request's budget. The app decorators counted one
way; the early gateway rules counted another. A user well under the *intended* allowance
could be rejected because each layer had independently spent part of their budget. False
confidence everywhere: each layer "looked fine" in isolation, the combined behavior was wrong.

## Root cause

We had **two rate-limiting systems** enforcing overlapping rules on the same traffic. Beyond
the double-counting, the app-layer approach was structurally wasteful: by the time a request
reaches a view decorator it has already opened a socket, completed TLS, been parsed, and
spawned a worker — we burn ~40ms of expensive compute just to say "no." At 12k req/s of
abuse, "rejected in the application" still saturates the worker pool. The emergency
decorators were a bandage, never the architecture.

## The lesson / what we decided

There must be **exactly one** place that enforces a request budget, and it must be at the
edge — the gateway — where an abusive request is dropped in <1ms before it touches a worker,
a connection, or any application resource. Two overlapping enforcement points is how you get
inconsistent behavior and false confidence; consolidating to one is how you get a single,
predictable budget per client.

Operational bonus: with enforcement in gateway config, changing a limit is an edit-and-reload,
no code deploy, no test cycle, no PR — oncall can adjust at 3am without waking a dev.

**Adding a throttle decorator or framework throttle class to a public endpoint is banned.**
The emergency decorators must be removed the moment the gateway rules land — leaving them in
is precisely what recreated this duplicated-budget incident. If you are tempted to add one,
you are looking at the wrong layer: write a gateway rule instead.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-19-gateway-rate-limit.md
- assumption: two overlapping enforcement layers double-count one client's budget; holds whenever a second rate-limit layer is introduced alongside the gateway
- component: api_gateway
issues.author: Notchayan

_note: API-sourced snapshot (offline replay). Regenerated by scripts/seed_demo_repo.py.

incoming.title: tune: raise Celery worker prefetch and add index on orders.created_at
incoming.text:
# PR #92: tune: raise Celery worker prefetch and add index on orders.created_at

**Author:** @daniel-osei
**Branch:** tune-workers → main

## Description

Two small performance refinements, both consistent with how the system already works: give the queue workers a higher prefetch so the email backlog drains faster after bursts, and index `orders.created_at` for the ops dashboard's date-range queries.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/email_service/worker.py
+++ b/email_service/worker.py
@@
-app.conf.worker_prefetch_multiplier = 1
+app.conf.worker_prefetch_multiplier = 4  # drain bursts faster

--- a/migrations/0044_orders_created_idx.py
+++ b/migrations/0044_orders_created_idx.py
@@
+migrations.AddIndex("Order", models.Index(fields=["created_at"]))
```

incoming.title: fix: handle missing shipping address in order summary
incoming.text:
# PR #90: fix: handle missing shipping address in order summary

**Author:** @new-contributor
**Branch:** fix-none-address → main

## Description

The order-summary page 500s for orders created before shipping addresses became mandatory. Guard against the missing relation and show a placeholder.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/orders/services.py
+++ b/orders/services.py
@@ def order_summary(order):
-    address = format_address(order.shipping_address)
+    if order.shipping_address is None:
+        address = "(no shipping address on file)"
+    else:
+        address = format_address(order.shipping_address)
```

incoming.title: docs: fix typos in README and code comments
incoming.text:
# PR #88: docs: fix typos in README and code comments

**Author:** @first-time-contributor
**Branch:** fix-typos → main

## Description

Small documentation cleanup: spelling fixes in the README and two code comments. No behavior change.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/README.md
+++ b/README.md
@@
-Recieve order confirmations after checkout.
+Receive order confirmations after checkout.

--- a/orders/services.py
+++ b/orders/services.py
@@
-# calcuate the order total including tax
+# calculate the order total including tax
```

incoming.title: Handle provider callbacks in the request — remove the handoff indirection
incoming.text:
# PR #87: Handle provider callbacks in the request — remove the handoff indirection

**Author:** @new-contributor
**Branch:** inline-callbacks → main

## Description

Tracing a callback today means hopping from the endpoint to a stored row to a background worker to the actual logic. This PR does the work right in the view: when the provider calls us, we update the order and write the ledger entry before responding. One stack trace, no indirection, easier debugging.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/webhooks/views.py
+++ b/webhooks/views.py
@@ def provider_callback(request):
     verify_signature(request)
-    RawEvent.objects.create(payload=request.body)
-    return Response(status=204)  # worker picks it up from here
+    event = parse_event(request.body)
+    apply_order_transition(event)      # do it now, in-request
+    write_ledger_entry(event)
+    notify_customer(event)
+    return Response(status=200)

--- a/webhooks/tasks.py
+++ b/webhooks/tasks.py
@@
-@queue.task
-def process_raw_event(event_id):
-    ...
```

incoming.title: Search the live product tables so results are always fresh
incoming.text:
# PR #86: Search the live product tables so results are always fresh

**Author:** @another-contributor
**Branch:** fresh-search → main

## Description

Merchandising noticed a product edit can take up to a minute to appear in search. Rather than tuning the copy pipeline, this PR queries the source-of-truth tables directly with the ORM — results are correct the instant anything changes, and we get to delete a background job.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/search/views.py
+++ b/search/views.py
@@ def search(request):
-    rows = SearchReadModel.objects.filter(text__search=q)
+    rows = (Product.objects
+        .select_related("pricing", "inventory", "brand")
+        .prefetch_related("categories__tree", "variants__options")
+        .filter(name__icontains=q))
     return Response(serialize(rows))

--- a/jobs/sync_read_model.py
+++ b/jobs/sync_read_model.py
@@
-def sync_read_model(event):
-    ...
```

incoming.title: Simplify database config — connect workers straight to the database
incoming.text:
# PR #85: Simplify database config — connect workers straight to the database

**Author:** @new-contributor
**Branch:** remove-db-proxy → main

## Description

We run an extra proxy container between the app and the database. It's one more thing to deploy, monitor, and debug through — and prepared statements need a workaround in its transaction mode. This PR removes the middleman: workers talk to the database directly, which is simpler and shaves a network hop.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/config/settings.py
+++ b/config/settings.py
@@ DATABASES = {
-        "HOST": "pgbouncer",
-        "PORT": 6432,
+        "HOST": "postgres",
+        "PORT": 5432,
+        "CONN_MAX_AGE": 600,  # keep connections warm per worker

--- a/docker-compose.yml
+++ b/docker-compose.yml
@@ services:
-  pgbouncer:
-    image: edoburu/pgbouncer:latest
-    volumes: [./infra/pgbouncer.ini:/etc/pgbouncer/pgbouncer.ini]
```

incoming.title: Clean up the capture endpoint — remove the dedupe bookkeeping
incoming.text:
# PR #84: Clean up the capture endpoint — remove the dedupe bookkeeping

**Author:** @another-contributor
**Branch:** simplify-capture → main

## Description

Integrators keep tripping over the mandatory header on the capture call — it's the top integration-support complaint. The extra table and unique index also add a write to our hottest path. This PR drops the requirement and the bookkeeping table; the endpoint just forwards the capture to the processor.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/payments/capture.py
+++ b/payments/capture.py
@@ def capture(request):
-    key = require_idempotency_key(request)
-    prior = IdempotencyRecord.objects.filter(key=key).first()
-    if prior:
-        return Response(prior.response_snapshot, status=prior.status_code)
     result = processor.capture(request.data["payment_id"], request.data["amount"])
-    IdempotencyRecord.objects.create(key=key, response_snapshot=result.json())
     return Response(result.json(), status=201)

--- a/payments/models.py
+++ b/payments/models.py
@@
-class IdempotencyRecord(models.Model):
-    key = models.CharField(max_length=64, unique=True)
-    response_snapshot = models.JSONField()
```

incoming.title: Persist user sessions in the database for instant revocation
incoming.text:
# PR #83: Persist user sessions in the database for instant revocation

**Author:** @new-contributor
**Branch:** db-backed-sessions → main

## Description

Support keeps asking for a "log this user out everywhere" button. The cleanest way is classic server-side sessions: keep a row per session so an admin can delete it and the user is out immediately. This PR switches auth back to Django's DB session backend and looks the session up on each request — standard, boring, battle-tested.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/config/settings.py
+++ b/config/settings.py
@@
+SESSION_ENGINE = "django.contrib.sessions.backends.db"
+MIDDLEWARE.insert(0, "django.contrib.sessions.middleware.SessionMiddleware")

--- a/auth/middleware.py
+++ b/auth/middleware.py
@@ def authenticate(request):
-    claims = verify_token_signature(request.headers["Authorization"])
-    request.user = User.from_claims(claims)
+    # look the session up so admins can revoke it row-by-row
+    session = Session.objects.get(session_key=request.COOKIES["sessionid"])
+    request.user = session.user
```

incoming.title: Throttle the orders & checkout endpoints in the app
incoming.text:
# PR #63: Throttle the orders & checkout endpoints in the app

**Author:** @another-contributor
**Branch:** throttle-orders → main

## Description

Following the same idea as the search throttling, this PR protects the `/orders` and
`/checkout` endpoints by adding DRF throttle classes / `@ratelimit` decorators on the
views. Limiting in application code keeps the rules close to the handlers and avoids
touching the gateway config, which most of us don't have access to.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/orders/views.py
+++ b/orders/views.py
@@
+from django_ratelimit.decorators import ratelimit
+
+@ratelimit(key="user_or_ip", rate="20/s", block=True)
 def list_orders(request):
     return Response(get_orders(request.user))

--- a/checkout/views.py
+++ b/checkout/views.py
@@
+from rest_framework.throttling import ScopedRateThrottle
+
 class CheckoutView(APIView):
+    throttle_classes = [ScopedRateThrottle]
+    throttle_scope = "checkout"
     def post(self, request):
         return Response(create_order(request.user, request.cart), status=201)
```

incoming.title: Add rate limiting to the search & products endpoints
incoming.text:
# PR #61: Add rate limiting to the search & products endpoints

**Author:** @new-contributor
**Branch:** add-search-throttle → main

## Description

The `/search` and `/products` endpoints occasionally get hammered by scrapers. This PR
adds `@ratelimit` decorators directly on the Django views so abusive clients get a 429.
It keeps the limiting logic right next to the business code, which is easy to read and
tweak per-view — no extra infrastructure to configure.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/search/views.py
+++ b/search/views.py
@@
+from django_ratelimit.decorators import ratelimit
+
+@ratelimit(key="ip", rate="30/s", block=True)
 def search(request):
     results = run_search(request.GET["q"])
     return Response(results)

--- a/products/views.py
+++ b/products/views.py
@@
+from django_ratelimit.decorators import ratelimit
+
+@ratelimit(key="ip", rate="60/s", block=True)
 def products(request):
     return Response(list_products(request.GET))

--- a/requirements.txt
+++ b/requirements.txt
@@
+django-ratelimit==4.1.0
```

incoming.title: Simplify checkout — send confirmation email synchronously
incoming.text:
# PR #57: Simplify checkout — send confirmation email synchronously

**Author:** @new-contributor
**Branch:** simplify-email → main

## Description

The Celery + Redis setup for sending confirmation emails feels like overkill and
adds moving parts to deploy. This PR simplifies it: the checkout endpoint now sends
the confirmation email directly via SendGrid SMTP, inline, before returning. One
fewer service to run, easier to reason about.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/checkout/views.py
+++ b/checkout/views.py
@@ def checkout(request):
         order = create_order(request.user, request.cart)
-        # hand off email to the background queue
-        send_order_confirmation_task.delay(order.id)
+        # send the confirmation email directly — simpler, no queue needed
+        send_email_smtp(order.user.email, render_confirmation(order))
         return Response({"order_id": order.id}, status=201)

--- a/checkout/tasks.py
+++ b/checkout/tasks.py
@@
-@celery_app.task(bind=True, max_retries=3)
-def send_order_confirmation_task(self, order_id):
-    ...
```
